title: Portland City Council Work Session FY 26-27 Budget Discussion  3/19/26

chapters.summary: Fourth in a series of work sessions on the upcoming budget process. Today we're going to be hearing about the Public Works Service Area. We'll cover 4 areas at today's presentation at a very high level. We welcome your questions and feedback as we move through the presentation.
chapters.headline: This is the fourth in a series of briefings on the upcoming budget process

chapters.summary: Public Works manages large, aging infrastructure systems. Cost of maintaining them is growing faster than the revenues that support them. This creates a structural gap between the service level expectations and the revenue available to support these services. These will be the central policy questions before council for the Public Works this budget cycle.
chapters.headline: Public Works faces significant pressures to balance service needs and declining funding

chapters.summary: The Public Works service area delivers core services Portlanders rely on every day. Across the 4 Public Works bureaus, we manage an annual budget of approximately $4.7 billion. $14.2 billion in deferred maintenance across the entire service area. The decision made in this budget cycle will shape asset condition, system reliability, and service delivery for decades.
chapters.headline: The Public Works service area delivers core services Portlanders rely on every day

chapters.summary: This slide highlights a few performance measures across the Public Works bureaus. These measures show what the Portlanders experience every day and what it takes to deliver that level of service reliably. Maintenance, staffing, and operations makes possible every day.
chapters.headline: This slide highlights a few performance measures across the Public Works bureaus

chapters.summary: Public Works expenses largely reflect the cost of operating and maintaining major infrastructure. The increase that you see in the last couple of years is primarily in the water fund. Inflation in labor and materials is impacting all of us across the city. As a result, operating costs are rising faster than many of our revenue sources.
chapters.headline: This chart shows program expenses by fund across the Public Works Service Area

chapters.summary: Public Works has a complicated revenue structure made up of multiple funding sources. Most of these funds are restricted to specific purposes. As a result, there's limited flexibility to move these resources across systems or bureaus.
chapters.headline: Public Works has a complicated revenue structure made up of multiple funding sources

chapters.summary: For transportation, the general fund makes up less than 1% of the total budget. About 45% of parks funding comes from general fund, and that support is also declining over time. General fund decisions have significant impact in addressing parks' service level gap.
chapters.headline: About 45% of parks funding comes from general fund and that support is declining
chapters.gist: Public Works Service Area General Fund

chapters.summary: Over the past 5 years, staffing level across Public Works have remained relatively flat and in some cases have slightly declined. Service expectations and infrastructure demands continue to increase. Further reductions to the workforce would directly impact the services that the bureaus are able to provide.
chapters.headline: Staffing levels across Public Works have remained relatively flat over the past 5 years

chapters.summary: The largest expenditures are focused on core infrastructure functions such as water treatment, wastewater capital investment, and transportation construction. When maintenance is deferred, the operating cost tends to go up because systems become more expensive to repair and maintain over time. Policy choices available to address these gaps come down to adjusting revenue or adjusting service levels.
chapters.headline: This slide highlights the gap between the revenue available and the cost to maintain Portland infrastructure

chapters.summary: The first is operational optimization, and we at Public Works have prioritized efficiencies first. The other path is revenue adjustments, which includes adjusting rates or fees where the tools exist. Neither option is easy in a service area which is as visible and as essential as public works.
chapters.headline: This slide shows the primary levers available to close budget gaps across the service area
chapters.gist: Public Works Budget: Four Ways to Close the Budget Gap

chapters.summary: Water and BES are experiencing pressures largely from inflation in operating costs and capital obligations. PBOT faces the largest gap, about $25 million in general transportation revenue. The choices to address these gaps ultimately come down to how we balance the service levels and revenue within each bureau's funding structure.
chapters.headline: This slide summarizes the drivers and magnitude of each bureau's gap and policy options

chapters.summary: The water system is about $2.2 billion in resources for the fiscal year '27. Funding supports day-to-day operations, major capital investments, and debt service for infrastructure projects. Most of these resources are already committed to operating, maintaining, and financing critical infrastructure.
chapters.headline: This slide shows where the Water Bureau's funding comes from and how it is used
chapters.gist: Water System Funding and How It is Used

chapters.summary: 3 scenarios for balancing the Water Bureau's budget. Each reflecting a different balance between rate affordability, service levels, and long-term costs. Decision in front of Council is how to balance rate affordability and service levels.
chapters.headline: This slide outlines 3 scenarios for balancing the Water Bureau's budget

chapters.summary: Nearly 90% of BES revenue comes from utility rates. These rates support day-to-day operations, system maintenance, emergency repair, and major capital investment. Most of the system's funding is already committed to maintaining critical infrastructure, leaving very limited room for reductions.
chapters.headline: Nearly 90% of BES revenue comes from utility rates

chapters.summary: Each scenario has a different balance between rate affordability, service levels, and long-term system performance. Together, the utilities manage about $52 billion in infrastructure with nearly $3 billion in planned capital investment over the next 5 years.
chapters.headline: BES is balancing affordability with maintaining service levels and system reliability

chapters.summary: Annual rate adjustments are necessary to keep the pace with inflation ongoing operations and infrastructure maintenance, as well as delivery of capital plans. Affordability remains a key priority for our utility bureaus. Rate projections are developed to balance system needs with impacts on households.
chapters.headline: Next slide shows the 5-year rate forecast for water and PES

chapters.summary: The combined rate increase for Scenario 2 is in the mid-6% over the next 4 years, and that is utilizing the 40-year bond. Longer-term bonds also saddles future ratepayers with more debt over time. We have robust financial assistance programs in place to support customers who need help managing costs.
chapters.headline: Next slide shows proposed rate increases for water and BES
chapters.gist: Water and BES Rate Analysis under Scenarios 1 and 2

chapters.summary: A common misconception is that Portland has some of the highest utility rates in the region or even in the country. Despite cost pressures, Portland utilities rates remain within the range of comparable national peer cities. About $39 million was dedicated to support financial assistance and affordability programs last year.
chapters.headline: Despite cost pressures, Portland utilities rates remain within comparable peer cities
chapters.gist: Portland Utilities: Rate stability and affordability

chapters.summary: General transportation revenue, or GTR, represents PBOT's most flexible funding source. The bottom portion of the chart represents restricted funding, which totals about to about $381 million. That is where the bureau's structural funding challenges occur.
chapters.headline: Only a portion of PBOT's budget is flexible and that is where structural challenges occur

chapters.summary: For PBOT, the most significant issue is a projected $25 million gap in the general transportation revenue over the 5-year financial plan. Addressing this gap will require a combination of service reductions, program adjustments, and/or new revenue sources.
chapters.headline: PBOT projects $25 million gap in general transportation revenue over 5-year plan
chapters.gist: Budget Shortfalls at the City of Denver

chapters.summary: Most of our operating budget in Parks is concentrated in the general fund. That includes about $82 million in general fund support, $78 million from the parks levy, and about $19 million in earned revenue from programs and fees. However, maintaining current service levels requires about $185 million.
chapters.headline: Most of our operating budget in Parks is concentrated in the general fund

chapters.summary: Rising costs and potential general fund reductions remain a possibility and would directly affect service levels. The largest drivers are internal service costs and employee health benefits. Next slide shows what happens to park's financial outlook under current service levels compared to making reductions.
chapters.headline: Rising costs and potential general fund reductions could affect service levels

chapters.summary: Parks looked for operational efficiencies first, and then prioritized protecting core services. Reduction options were done using clear criteria aligned with the city's core values. This approach ensures that decisions are not made in isolation, but reflect community priorities and longstanding policy direction.
chapters.headline: Parks is considering a general fund reduction of up to $8.1 million
chapters.gist: Parks Budget Review and Potential Reduction

chapters.summary: The Bureau developed nearly 5 dozen potential reduction options across the 3 scenarios. The impacts focus primarily on operational adjustments and reduced capacity rather than visible closures. Under the 10% reduction scenario, the impacts become more visible to the public.
chapters.headline: The Bureau developed nearly 5 dozen potential park service reduction options

chapters.summary: Key takeaways across Public Works bureaus. We are managing essential systems where costs continue to rise. Decreased service levels also come with a responsibility to manage expectations with Portlanders. The decisions made in this budget cycle will shape service reliability, affordability, and asset condition for years to come.
chapters.headline: Service level adjustments and revenue adjustments are critical decisions for Portland Public Works

chapters.summary: Council President: Public Works is the most basic area of city services. He says the maintenance backlog is difficult to fathom and how we're going to address that. Council President: We risk really being quite liable in the public.
chapters.headline: Council President Danipaul raises concerns about maintenance backlog in Public Works

chapters.summary: On the Water Bureau, we have talked about getting an outside independent review of the water filtration plant. Director Lou is hoping to provide an update to the council in an upcoming T&I committee. We'll also be scheduling a discussion of the utility rates, and colleagues.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green has a couple questions about the Water Bureau and utilities

chapters.summary: Councilor Green: You got to pay for the streets. deferred maintenance creates an upward cost pressure bias on operation and maintenance. Whether we're rate basing it all the way right now or we are financing it through long-term bond issuances, we have to think about the generations that follow us.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green: I appreciate the presentation on deferred maintenance for Portland streets

chapters.summary: But I do have a specific question on slide 6 and also, I guess, 7. Can you speak a little bit about what was being transferred from which fund to what looks like the Public Works Service Area?
chapters.headline: I have a specific question about fund transfers on slide 6 and 7
chapters.gist: Fund Transferments to the Public Works Service Area

chapters.summary: The fund transfers represent the internal movement of resources between funds for financial management and transfers to or from the rate stabilization funds. A large part of the fund transfers are actually within water and within BES. The increase that you're seeing there is the increase in our capital program.
chapters.headline: Fund transfers represent movement of resources between funds for financial management and rate stabilization funds
chapters.gist: Fund Transferments for Water and BES

chapters.summary: Councillor: I'm not supportive of Scenario 3. This is going to increase the ongoing operations and maintenance liabilities and our system resiliency liabilities. The decision that me as a councillor in one office of 12 has to think through is Do I support scenario 1 or scenario 2?
chapters.headline: Council member is not supportive of Scenario 3 for water rates

chapters.summary: I think it's worth just maybe sharing how I think about the 40-year bond trade-off versus the 30. I'm supporting Scenario 2. I would like to know more about what some of these service impacts are that are on there.
chapters.headline: I think it's worth just maybe sharing how I think about the 40-year bond trade-off

chapters.summary: Parks and Rec budget scenarios. I was having trouble reading, making the connection between the scenarios and the reduction levels. Is it just that the service level gap is maybe more than 10% or it's more than 3%?
chapters.headline: I had trouble reading some of the Parks and Rec budget scenarios

chapters.summary: Mayor: We're looking at every part of the city for savings. Union says there's concern that we are not doing our due diligence on cost control. What is the true cost of our contracting out program? Mayor: We can get back to you with the actual contract numbers.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green wants to know what the true cost of contracting out is
chapters.gist: Councillor Green on cost control at PBOT

chapters.summary: On slide number 4, talking about the maintenance backlog, I am wondering if there is an ability to break this backlog down by district. PBOT calculates maintenance as being more expensive the further from the central city it is. Once we collect that information, we're happy to bring it back.
chapters.headline: On slide number 4, talking about the maintenance backlog, I am wondering if there is

chapters.summary: Council President: Historically, it costs more to drive out further distances. But looking holistically, it's really the age of that infrastructure. East Portland probably has the youngest sewer pipes. I'm interested to know more.
chapters.headline: Council President: Age of infrastructure is what drives repair costs
chapters.gist: East Portland vs. Downtown Portland: Why Does It Cost More to

chapters.summary: Last year, this council raised the hourly cost for parking. Did that lead to lower collections? Did we scare people away? Is there a break-even point, or do they ultimately pay for themselves?
chapters.headline: On slide 13, there's a statement about declining parking revenue

chapters.summary: Mayor: I'm more concerned about being better than Gresham and Happy Valley. On slide 25, there are things that I'd like to see broken down by district wherever possible. With regard to parks and the likelihood that we are going to have to reduce some services, why would we build it now?
chapters.headline: Councilor: We are significantly ahead neighboring cities in terms of our overall rate
chapters.gist: Portland City Council Discuss Peer City List

chapters.summary: Do we know how much it cost and how much regular citizens were paying when we first built the water system? And on parking revenues, have those numbers kept on going down even during the recovery? They have been going up, but mostly that is due to the rate increases.
chapters.headline: Parking revenues have been going up, but mostly due to rate increases
chapters.gist: Portland Parking revenues and the cost of the system

chapters.summary: $45 million goes to street sweeping, graffiti, derelict RV, campsite cleanup. $25 million for project delivery. $20-25 million of that is pavement. I'd like to see a breakdown of that too.
chapters.headline: Priya Novick: I'm curious about PBOT's GTR flexible spending
chapters.gist: PBOT Capital Spending, GTR Flexible Spending

chapters.summary: Councilor Green asked why the budget slides add up to $2.04 billion but the revenue side's come up to$4.78 billion. The slides are actually showing two different things as far as— so the resources.
chapters.headline: Councilor Green asked about program expenses and revenue on two slides
chapters.gist: Budget and Program Expenses on the Same Slide

chapters.summary: Mayor: To make Portlanders more aware of the option to seek a monthly bill versus a quarterly one. On the subject of discounts, I think talking about how we do the discounts is really important to me. And happy to partner with you counselors if you want to share affordability programs.
chapters.headline: Portland councillors discuss plans to increase awareness of monthly bills versus quarterly bills
chapters.gist: Portland Water Board Discusses Affordability Programs

chapters.summary: So slide 28, you have, uh, I just wanted to understand the internal service fund forecasts. That's a pretty significant change in 6 months. And we're looking at 17% increases year over year through the forecast. We're working with CBO and with risk to try and determine whether there's some opportunity to bend that forecast down.
chapters.headline: I just wanted to understand the internal service fund, uh, forecasts
chapters.gist: CBO on Internal Service Fund Forecasts

chapters.summary: Councillor Knoll: Some reductions to recreation and community centers. It's not just community centers, but across the board and all the programs. Would the average voter have thought that if they approved the parks levy, this wasn't going to happen?
chapters.headline: Councilor Knoll raises concerns about possible reductions to recreation and community centers
chapters.gist: Discussion of possible cuts to parks programs

chapters.summary: Council President Dunphy talked about deferred maintenance backlog by district. He said many of our capital needs have been deferred because the resources have been focused on the filtration plant. That means the backlogs do not capture the full amount of necessary capital investments.
chapters.headline: Council President Dunphy talks about deferred maintenance backlog by district

chapters.summary: On the subject of parking enforcement, you said that if we had no enforcement, we'd have nobody paying parking rates. I would love to understand the conversation on enforcement on double parking for loading vehicles. Director Williams is here to answer your— respond to your question.
chapters.headline: On the subject of parking enforcement, you said that if we had no enforcement, we'd have no rates

chapters.summary: Portland Bureau of Transportation director: Compliance is up 70%, but revenue has to catch up. Councilor: We would like to incentivize your parking enforcement officers to go after people double parking in loading zones. Council President Kunal Kunal: Maybe we could deputize a vigilante parking officer.
chapters.headline: Millicent Williams: Compliance with parking regulations up 70% over last few years
chapters.gist: Parking and loading zones, as reviewed by Portland Bureau of Transportation

chapters.summary: Councilor Pirtle-Guinea: It doesn't necessarily make sense to apply every 25% rule to everything. He says it's worth having a conversation around a 15 or 20% understanding. Councilor Knoll: I would love to have that conversation with colleagues going forward.
chapters.headline: Council President says 25% of city investment should be in every district

chapters.summary: The typical lifespan of infrastructure asset like that for the inner water bureau is about 50 to 100 years. In addition to that new infrastructure, that there's going to be ongoing maintenance. A thought is to spread the cost to the future years as much as possible.
chapters.headline: We're talking about bonding for a water treatment facility

chapters.summary: I'm interested in understanding what the rates would look like if we looked at the reductions in Scenario 2 but a 30-year bond. The longer we push out our bond payments, the more we are depending on population growth. I don't want to set us up for a long-term cycle of chasing growth in an unsustainable way.
chapters.headline: Councilor: I'm interested in understanding what the rates would look like

chapters.summary: year 1, and then the forecast will be $9.8 million. So it would give us a slight decrease in the, in the increase in year 1, but we would still be looking at the same numbers lowered by just a couple of dollars from that first year out from there.
chapters.headline: It sounds like doing a partial scenario one is better than a full scenario one
chapters.gist: The cost to taxpayers of the tax increase

chapters.summary: I am also wondering about the ratepayer stability fund. Over the past 8 or 10 years, those reserves are decent, not huge, and relatively stable. Is that being drawn on in Scenario 2 as well, or are we able to move forward without drawing on those funds?
chapters.headline: I am wondering about the ratepayer stability fund. Are there triggers for when we can access those funds
chapters.gist: Questions about the Ratepayer Stability Fund

chapters.summary: Councillor: I want to understand more what the reductions in recreation community centers and environmental education are more specifically. When we get specifics, I will be asking very specifically about summer programming, affordable camp options.
chapters.headline: Councillor: I have a couple of questions about parks and environmental education
chapters.gist: Council Discusses Parks and Community Centers

chapters.summary: Is that deficit line parks levy or full parks funding? I am concerned that in both of these slides we show a deficit in the final year of the levy. I don't think it's acceptable just a few months after we asked Portlanders to fund a sustainable level of levy.
chapters.headline: Both slides show a deficit in the final year of the levy
chapters.gist: Questions about the proposed levy for parks

chapters.summary: Year over year still shows a deficit in that final year even with the $7 million reduction. And that's the piece that's concerning to me. And I think we have a lot of other work that will happen around our transportation funding.
chapters.headline: Year over year deficit still shows in final year even with $7 million reductions

chapters.summary: Councilor Clark: I'm quite concerned that on slide 31 that we're looking at reducing more of the parks maintenance budget. And my second comment is, about not so much volunteerism but entrepreneurial approach to our assets. Let's not be penny-wise and pound-foolish.
chapters.headline: Councilor Ryan expresses concern about proposed cuts to parks maintenance budget
chapters.gist: Councilors: Concerns about parks maintenance budget

chapters.summary: Council President: This year it can no longer be talk. Public works and public safety is our must-haves. And so by having the scenario planning slides, it brought that reality. I hope that we can prepare, we can protect as much wisdom on the ground.
chapters.headline: Council President Diopold: This year's budget involves difficult tradeoffs

chapters.summary: Jonas Beery, the city's chief financial officer. Could you come up and give us bold, direct, crisp— on what is it, what's really the impacts of 40-year Bond compared to 30-year bond.
chapters.headline: Councillor asks question about 40-year bond compared to 30-year

chapters.summary: I appreciate and support the filtration system project. Many Portlanders are struggling right now with affordability. I hope with that we see creative, bold restructuring.
chapters.headline: Councilor Clark supports the city's water filtration project
chapters.gist: Portland Water Bureau filtration system project

chapters.summary: On parks, first I want to applaud you for hiring someone to focus on philanthropic efforts. All of our assets that are facing the public right away are experiencing more damage. That's just a fact. And it's making our cost drivers go up and expenses go up.
chapters.headline: On parks, first I want to applaud you for hiring someone to focus on philanthropic efforts

chapters.summary: I am intrigued by how you got to your budget approach. Was that predominantly with the Parks Board? Was it with the Friends groups? Who were the voices at the table? Table that inspired this approach to budget prioritization.
chapters.headline: I am curious about how you got to your budget approach

chapters.summary: Great question. We had very little time to develop scenarios for this process. We worked primarily among our senior management team. We did lean heavily though on prior community engagement, including very recent.
chapters.headline: Sonia Schimanski: We had very little time to develop scenarios
chapters.gist: Questions about the Parks Bureau's proposed budget

chapters.summary: Council President: The pool was selected at that site because it was by far the highest equity-rated site of the 3 locations. Councilor Green: We have to think of it as a system to improve swim literacy. Councilman: Our bread and butter revenue for general fund is way down.
chapters.headline: Council President Ryan says swim literacy is key goal of proposed Portland pool
chapters.gist: Council discusses swim literacy at the pool

chapters.summary: Councilor Green: If the choice is between not issuing the bonds and paying for A, always don't issue the bonds. Research shows that when you have a density-focused growth regime, that your cost per unit of delivery of infrastructure declines. Council's ability to issue bonds is a function of creditworthiness, he says.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick weighed in on the debate around Portland's debt

chapters.summary: On the proposed parks cuts, I think that the biggest chunks of parks expenses are maintenance of the outdoor parks in particular and operation of the community centers and other facilities. It might be reasonable to sort of look at 2 approaches. One, cutting back on maintenance. Two, cuttingback on operations.
chapters.headline: On the proposed parks cuts, Um, I think the biggest chunks of expenses are maintenance

chapters.summary: Councilor Novick: It would take an additional $40 to $50 million a year to keep our streets of citywide concern in their current mediocre condition. He says parks are not a luxury, they are essential infrastructure. Councilor Avalos: We can't balance this budget on the backs of residents.
chapters.headline: Councilor Novick says $7 million gap for parks could mean service cuts
chapters.gist: Portland budget: Streets of Citywide Concern, parks funding

chapters.summary: DCA Donegal had a follow-up question about the filtration project. You'd mentioned an independent financial oversight Committee. Are there other accountability or cost control measures the Bureau is working on? I'm going to call Director Lu to talk to that one.
chapters.headline: Donna Paul: I had a follow-up question about the filtration project
chapters.gist: Independent Financial Oversight for the Filtration Project

chapters.summary: Transparency and accountability is a top priority for us for the filtration project. I'm currently working with the project staff to evaluating existing measures. Will soon share a plan for additional independent financial oversight to you all.
chapters.headline: Ting Lu: Transparency and accountability are top priorities for Portland filtration project
chapters.gist: Portland Water Bureau's filtration project transparency

chapters.summary: Public Works has been focused on One Water and unified CIP moving forward. The teams are already working on multiple of these initiatives together. In the upcoming few months, we will be coming to the Public Works Committee to share an update.
chapters.headline: What's the status on One Water and unified CIP moving forward

chapters.summary: Next, I'm going to invite, uh, CFO Jonas Beery up. We're going to discuss about what is coming next, what the process is beyond today. To make adequate space to support council decision-making related to the annual budget. Public testimony is required for the mayor's proposed hearing on April 21st, and approved budget hearing on May 18th.
chapters.headline: Council President outlines upcoming public meetings related to the city budget
chapters.gist: City Council: Budget Committee and Public testimony

chapters.summary: April 21st will be the mayor's proposed hearing. There's an adjacent moment on April 21st related to the budget for Prosper Portland Council. Next series of work sessions are kind of the next deep dive. Having upfront clarity about timelines and plans is immensely helpful.
chapters.headline: Council's role as relates to Prosper's budget is twofold
chapters.gist: Planning and Budget for Prosper Portland Council

chapters.summary: The week of May 18th will be the approved budget hearing. The key pieces of that will be to discuss amendments to the proposed budget and to take public testimony. 3 key things will need to occur before that hearing ends. Council will approve the city's budget and then vote on the utility rate ordinance.
chapters.headline: The week of May 18th will be the approved budget hearing

chapters.summary: The continued continuation of the budget hearing to the extent needed through May 20th. And then we've got a placeholder work session on June 3rd if we need space for to continue dialogue between the approved budget and the budget adoption in June. It is critical that we meet that final date and have a balanced city budget.
chapters.headline: Continuation of budget hearing through May 20th; adoption on June 10th
chapters.gist: The Process of the City Budget

chapters.summary: Councillors should anticipate a memo coming from me with detailed information about this, including all these dates. Mine is actually a plug for Parks. Next week is spring break, and you'll be having free lunch delivery services. Could it be possible to separate Portland Solutions and revenue generation?
chapters.headline: Council members take questions on Portland Solutions and revenue generation

chapters.summary: Perfect. With that, I'm going to adjourn this work session. 4:54. Thank you very much.
chapters.headline: All right. With that, I'm going to adjourn this work session
